stem: You are the community psychiatry registrar reviewing a 28-year-old man with schizophrenia who was started on olanzapine 10 mg nocte three months ago. He has had no further hallucinations and is sleeping well. He reports a 7 kg weight gain and increased thirst. His mother has type 2 diabetes. Baseline metabolic screen at olanzapine commencement showed fasting glucose 4.9 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.4%, total cholesterol 4.6 mmol/L, and waist circumference 88 cm. He has no chest pain and no polyuria overnight.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/82, HR 78, weight 87 kg (baseline 80 kg), waist 96 cm
data_table.Examination: Acanthosis nigricans absent, no peripheral oedema

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in metabolic monitoring?

options.text: Repeat fasting lipids, fasting glucose, HbA1c and waist circumference today and review
options.rationale: RANZCP and Australian Diabetes Society monitoring for second-generation antipsychotics recommends fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipids and waist at 3 months, then at least annually; weight gain at 3 months is the trigger to recheck full panel and consider intervention. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Defer metabolic bloods until the 12 month review since baseline was normal and recheck waist now
options.rationale: Deferring to 12 months ignores the recommended 3 month repeat for fasting glucose, HbA1c and lipids on olanzapine, which carries the highest metabolic risk of the SGAs. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Order random glucose, total cholesterol and weight only and review by phone in one week
options.rationale: Random glucose and isolated total cholesterol do not meet RANZCP monitoring standards, which specify fasting glucose plus HbA1c and a full fasting lipid panel. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Switch immediately to aripiprazole today and arrange repeat fasting bloods in one month
options.rationale: Switching without first quantifying metabolic change risks destabilising remission; rescreen first, then consider a lower metabolic risk SGA if the panel is deranged. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Start metformin 500 mg twice daily empirically and recheck waist circumference at 6 months
options.rationale: Metformin for antipsychotic-induced weight gain is reasonable in selected patients but is not first action before measuring fasting glucose and HbA1c to confirm dysglycaemia or prediabetes. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Olanzapine carries one of the highest metabolic risk profiles of all antipsychotics, with weight gain, dyslipidaemia and incident type 2 diabetes overrepresented compared with aripiprazole or lurasidone. Australian monitoring frameworks recommend a baseline metabolic panel, repeat at 3 months, then at least annually thereafter while on treatment, with more frequent monitoring if any parameter is abnormal or trending. A 7 kg gain by 3 months is a red flag for emerging metabolic syndrome and should drive a full repeat panel before adjusting therapy. Decisions about switching, adding metformin, or pursuing lifestyle interventions follow the panel, not precede it. Family history of diabetes raises pretest probability of progression.
key_points: Olanzapine and clozapine are the SGAs with highest metabolic burden
Monitor fasting glucose, HbA1c, fasting lipids, weight and waist at baseline, 3 months, then annually
>5 kg gain or >5% body weight gain at 3 months should trigger early intervention
Metformin and aripiprazole augmentation have evidence for SGA-induced weight gain
Family history of diabetes lowers the threshold for switching to a lower metabolic risk SGA
explanation.pearls: Olanzapine produces a mean weight gain of about 3 to 4 kg by 10 weeks in first-episode treatment; a 7 kg gain at 3 months sits well above average and predicts persistent metabolic harm if untreated.

stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 58-year-old woman on lithium carbonate 750 mg nocte for bipolar I disorder for 18 years. She had her last episode 6 years ago and remains euthymic. Routine bloods today show eGFR 47 mL/min/1.73 m2 (53 six months ago, 62 two years ago) with a lithium level of 0.78 mmol/L taken 12 hours post-dose. She takes ramipril for hypertension. She is fearful of relapse if lithium is stopped.

data_table.Examination: Fine tremor of outstretched hands, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 118, eGFR 47 (53 at 6 months, 62 at 24 months); lithium 0.78 mmol/L; TSH 3.1; corrected calcium 2.42
data_table.Medications: Lithium carbonate 750 mg nocte, ramipril 5 mg mane

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue lithium at current dose and recheck eGFR and lithium level in 12 months as planned
options.rationale: A sustained downward trend in eGFR over 24 months with eGFR now <60 mandates earlier review than annual, not routine continuation. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar CPG)

options.text: Refer to nephrology for shared review and discuss reducing lithium or switching mood stabiliser
options.rationale: Progressive eGFR decline (CKD stage 3a with downward trend) on long-term lithium warrants joint nephrology and psychiatry review; options include dose reduction with closer level monitoring or switch to valproate or lamotrigine after discussing relapse risk. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar CPG)

options.text: Stop lithium today and start sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily titrated against levels weekly
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium cessation increases relapse risk substantially; any switch should be planned with overlapping cover and patient consent after multidisciplinary review. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar CPG)

options.text: Reduce lithium to 500 mg nocte without further investigation and recheck level in two weeks
options.rationale: Unilateral dose reduction without nephrology input or imaging risks subtherapeutic levels and does not address possible structural lithium nephropathy. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar CPG)

options.text: Stop ramipril and recheck eGFR in two weeks since ACE inhibitors lower eGFR through haemodynamic effect
options.rationale: ACE inhibitor-mediated eGFR drop is usually small and stable; a progressive decline over 24 months is not explained by ramipril alone. (Source: KHA-CARI)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium produces a clinically meaningful chronic interstitial nephropathy in a minority of patients after 10 to 20 years, with the dominant risk factors being duration of exposure, episodes of toxicity, and concurrent nephrotoxins. RANZCP guidelines recommend renal function monitoring 6 monthly, with thyroid and calcium annually, and earlier review when eGFR trend declines or absolute eGFR drops below 60. Sustained decline below 60 with continued lithium use warrants shared nephrology review and a frank discussion of switching mood stabilisers, accepting elevated relapse risk especially in patients with multiple prior manic episodes. Lamotrigine and valproate are reasonable alternatives but neither equals lithium for suicide-reduction in bipolar disorder. Decisions hinge on the relapse vs renal risk trade-off and patient preference.
key_points: Lithium nephropathy is a chronic interstitial nephritis from cumulative exposure
RANZCP: eGFR, TSH and calcium 6 to 12 monthly on maintenance lithium
Sustained eGFR fall below 60 triggers nephrology referral and switch consideration
Abrupt lithium cessation has high (>50%) relapse risk over 12 months
Lithium retains a unique anti-suicide signal over valproate and lamotrigine
explanation.pearls: Cumulative lithium exposure over 15 years roughly doubles the risk of CKD stage 3, with the strongest predictor being prior episodes of lithium toxicity (level >1.5 mmol/L) rather than maintenance level.

subtopic_detail: Valproate in women of reproductive potential
stem: You are the early psychosis registrar reviewing a 23-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder commenced on sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily by the inpatient team three weeks ago following a manic episode. She is now euthymic. She is in a new sexual relationship and uses condoms only intermittently. She is not pregnant on testing today. She has no prior trial of lithium or other mood stabilisers and her mother had similar episodes managed with lithium.

data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative; valproate level 380 micromol/L; LFTs normal
data_table.Medications: Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily; combined OCP discontinued 2 years ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in mood stabiliser management?

options.text: Continue valproate at current dose with monthly pregnancy testing and folate 5 mg daily
options.rationale: Folate and monthly testing do not mitigate the 10% major congenital malformation rate or 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental harm if she conceives on valproate. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar CPG)

options.text: Continue valproate but add the combined oral contraceptive pill and review in three months
options.rationale: Even with reliable contraception the TGA and RANZCP advise against valproate in women of reproductive potential when alternatives exist, because unintended pregnancy still occurs and exposure is teratogenic from the first weeks. (Source: TGA Valproate Warning 2024)

options.text: Switch valproate to lithium with overlapping cover and arrange contraception counselling now
options.rationale: Valproate is contraindicated in women of reproductive potential unless no effective alternative exists and a pregnancy prevention programme is in place; lithium is a guideline-supported first-line maintenance option in bipolar I and aligns with her family history of response. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar CPG; TGA Valproate Warning 2024)

options.text: Reduce valproate to 250 mg twice daily as a lower teratogenic risk maintenance dose for now
options.rationale: Valproate teratogenicity is dose-related but no maintenance dose is considered safe in pregnancy; reduction does not solve the contraindication and risks relapse. (Source: TGA Valproate Warning 2024)

options.text: Continue valproate and refer to obstetrics for preconception counselling at her annual review
options.rationale: Preconception counselling does not address the immediate teratogenic exposure window if she conceives between reviews; switch first. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar CPG)

explanation.summary: Valproate exposure during pregnancy is associated with about a 10% rate of major congenital malformations and a 30 to 40% rate of neurodevelopmental impairment, including autism spectrum disorder and reduced IQ. The TGA strengthened warnings in 2024 to align with European regulators, recommending valproate is only used in women of reproductive potential where no effective alternative exists and only within a documented pregnancy prevention programme. For maintenance treatment of bipolar I disorder, lithium remains the first-line option in most women, with lamotrigine an alternative if depressive polarity predominates. Family history of lithium response is a relevant prognostic factor. Switching should occur with overlapping cover and lithium loading with weekly levels until therapeutic range is reached.
key_points: Valproate: 10% major malformation rate, 30-40% neurodevelopmental harm
TGA 2024: contraindicated in women of reproductive potential without pregnancy prevention programme
Lithium is the preferred maintenance switch in bipolar I, especially with family response
Lamotrigine is preferred if depressive polarity dominates
Switch with overlapping cover; do not stop valproate abruptly during ongoing manic stabilisation
explanation.pearls: Sodium valproate plasma levels above 700 micromol/L during the first trimester carry the highest neural tube defect risk, but no level is considered safe in early pregnancy.

stem: You are the emergency registrar in a metropolitan ED at 2 am. A 34-year-old man arrives 90 minutes after ingesting an estimated 60 of his amitriptyline 25 mg tablets with two cans of beer. He is drowsy but rousable. He has a history of major depressive disorder. He has no prior cardiac history. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 124 bpm with QRS 124 ms and a terminal R wave of 4 mm in aVR.

data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, sluggish pupils, hyporeflexic, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.32, pCO2 38, HCO3 19, K 3.4, Na 138; paracetamol level pending; ethanol 22 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: IV flumazenil 0.2 mg titrated to reversal of sedation and improve airway protection
options.rationale: Flumazenil in mixed overdose with TCAs precipitates seizures by unmasking proconvulsant effects; it is contraindicated in this setting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube with airway protection after sedation
options.rationale: Charcoal beyond one hour post-ingestion in a patient with reduced GCS increases aspiration risk and offers limited benefit; addressing cardiotoxicity is the immediate priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: IV intralipid 20% bolus 1.5 mL/kg with infusion 0.25 mL/kg/min over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Intralipid is reserved for cardiovascular collapse refractory to bicarbonate and standard resuscitation; it is not first-line for QRS prolongation alone. (Source: Australian Poisons Centre TCA Guideline)

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: QRS >100 ms (and especially >120 ms) plus terminal R wave in aVR confirms significant TCA cardiotoxicity, where bicarbonate boluses targeting pH 7.50 to 7.55 reduce sodium channel blockade and prevent ventricular arrhythmia. (Source: Australian Poisons Centre TCA Guideline)

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes with potassium replacement to 4.5 mmol/L target
options.rationale: Magnesium is useful for torsades or refractory ventricular arrhythmia but does not address the underlying sodium channel block driving QRS prolongation. (Source: Australian Poisons Centre TCA Guideline)

explanation.summary: TCA overdose toxicity is dominated by sodium channel blockade, anticholinergic features and alpha-1 antagonism, producing the classic constellation of QRS prolongation, tachycardia, hypotension, sedation and seizures. ECG predictors of serious toxicity include QRS >100 ms, terminal R wave >3 mm in aVR, and R/S ratio >0.7 in aVR. Sodium bicarbonate works by both increasing extracellular sodium gradient and alkalinising plasma to displace TCA from sodium channels; the target serum pH is around 7.50 to 7.55. Hypertonic saline is an alternative if bicarbonate is unavailable. Intralipid is reserved for refractory cardiovascular collapse and is not first-line. Seizures are managed with benzodiazepines, not phenytoin, which shares sodium-channel-blocking properties.
key_points: QRS >100 ms or terminal R wave aVR >3 mm = significant TCA cardiotoxicity
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg boluses target pH 7.50-7.55
Avoid flumazenil in mixed TCA overdose (seizure risk)
Avoid phenytoin for seizures - use benzodiazepines
Intralipid is rescue therapy for refractory collapse only
explanation.pearls: A terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm at presentation predicts seizure and arrhythmia in TCA overdose with about 80% sensitivity, outperforming QRS duration alone.

stem:
You are the inpatient psychiatry registrar reviewing a 32-year-old woman three weeks into admission. Her mood and energy have normalised on lithium and olanzapine after a manic episode characterised by reduced sleep, grandiose religious delusions and pressured speech. Her mood symptoms have settled but she continues to describe persecutory delusions about her neighbours bugging her flat and reports auditory hallucinations commenting on her movements.

There is a clear premorbid 9 month period over the past two years where she described persistent persecutory beliefs in the absence of any mood episode. She has been sleeping a full 8 hours nightly for the past 10 days.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: euthymic affect, no pressure of speech, persecutory delusions intact, auditory hallucinations present, insight partial
data_table.Medications: Lithium 750 mg nocte, olanzapine 15 mg nocte

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood congruent psychotic features in remission
options.rationale: Persistent psychotic symptoms outside mood episodes, especially the documented 9 month pre-manic persecutory delusions, are incompatible with bipolar I with mood-congruent features which confines psychosis to mood episodes. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder superimposed on bipolar I disorder
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder duration is <1 month with full return to premorbid function; this patient has had psychotic symptoms for months across separate periods. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia with comorbid substance-induced mood episode
options.rationale: Schizophrenia is not the best fit because the patient has had a sustained manic episode meeting DSM-5-TR criteria, which is not adequately captured by schizophrenia plus a substance-induced episode. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder with superimposed lithium-responsive mood episode
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires absence of marked impairment in functioning outside the delusion and absence of prominent hallucinations; auditory commentary makes this diagnosis untenable. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type with persistent psychotic features
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires psychotic symptoms for >=2 weeks in the absence of a mood episode (the pre-manic 9 month period) and mood episodes that occupy the majority of the total illness duration, with current persistent psychotic symptoms in remitted mood. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder is the diagnosis when DSM-5-TR criterion A for schizophrenia is met alongside mood episodes, AND psychotic symptoms persist for >=2 weeks in the absence of a major mood episode at some point in the illness, AND mood episodes occupy the majority of the total illness duration. The bipolar subtype requires a manic episode, while the depressive subtype requires only depressive episodes. Distinguishing schizoaffective from bipolar with mood-congruent psychotic features hinges on whether psychotic symptoms persist outside mood episodes; this is the single most discriminating feature. Treatment usually combines a mood stabiliser with a second-generation antipsychotic and carries an intermediate prognosis between bipolar I and schizophrenia. Cross-sectional review during one admission is rarely sufficient; the longitudinal history is decisive.
key_points: Schizoaffective dx requires >=2 weeks of psychosis without a mood episode
Mood episodes must occupy the MAJORITY of total illness duration
Bipolar I with mood-congruent psychosis confines psychosis to mood episodes
Delusional disorder excludes prominent hallucinations
Brief psychotic disorder = <1 month with full return to baseline
explanation.pearls: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type has prognosis closer to bipolar I than to schizophrenia, while the depressive type tracks closer to schizophrenia outcomes over 10 year follow-up.

stem: You are the GP registrar seeing a 48-year-old woman who smokes 20 cigarettes per day and wants to quit. She has tried nicotine patches before with only a 3 week period of abstinence. She has a history of major depressive disorder, currently in remission on sertraline 100 mg daily for 18 months. She has no history of seizures or eating disorder. She is not pregnant and does not breastfeed. She has no cardiovascular disease and no recent ACS. She wants the most effective option.

data_table.Investigations: Recent fasting lipids and HbA1c normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy to recommend?

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily titrated to 1 mg twice daily with set quit date 1 week in
options.rationale: Varenicline has the highest single-agent quit rate in head-to-head trials and is safe in stable depression on an SSRI; the EAGLES trial demonstrated no excess neuropsychiatric events compared with placebo. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Bupropion SR 150 mg daily titrated to twice daily with quit date set 1 week into therapy
options.rationale: Bupropion is an option but is contraindicated or relatively contraindicated with concurrent serotonergic medication due to seizure threshold and serotonergic load considerations, and is less effective than varenicline as monotherapy. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Single 21 mg nicotine patch daily with brief behavioural counselling for relapse prevention
options.rationale: Single-form NRT has lower efficacy than varenicline or combination NRT; she has already failed patches alone, so single patch is suboptimal as the next step. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Combination NRT with 21 mg patch and 4 mg gum as needed for cravings throughout the day
options.rationale: Combination NRT is more effective than single-form NRT and is a reasonable alternative, but evidence places varenicline ahead of combination NRT for quit rates in head-to-head trials. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Nortriptyline 25 mg nocte titrated up against tolerability with quit date set 2 weeks in
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is a second-line cessation aid not registered on PBS for this indication and risks serotonergic interaction with sertraline. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Australian guidelines and the 2024 Cochrane network meta-analysis place varenicline at the top of first-line smoking cessation pharmacotherapy, ahead of combination NRT and bupropion. Varenicline is a partial agonist at the alpha-4 beta-2 nicotinic receptor and approximately doubles continuous abstinence rates at 6 months compared with placebo. The 2016 EAGLES trial dispelled earlier concerns about neuropsychiatric events in stable depression and schizophrenia, allowing confident use in mental illness in remission. Bupropion lowers seizure threshold and has relative contraindications with concurrent SSRIs. NRT remains a valid first-line option, especially in combination, and is the default in pregnancy where varenicline is not recommended. Behavioural support roughly doubles the efficacy of any pharmacotherapy.
key_points: Varenicline > combination NRT > bupropion > single-form NRT (Cochrane 2024)
EAGLES trial: varenicline safe in stable depression and schizophrenia
Set quit date 1-2 weeks into varenicline; titrate over first week
Combination NRT (patch + short-acting) > patch alone
Add behavioural counselling (Quitline 13 7848) for additive effect
explanation.pearls: Varenicline reaches steady state at 4 days and is renally excreted; dose reduce to 1 mg daily when eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73 m2.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar in an adult ADHD clinic seeing a 29-year-old software engineer who self-referred. She describes lifelong difficulty starting tasks, frequent loss of personal items, frequent procrastination on important emails, and inability to follow long meetings. School reports from age 9 documented daydreaming and disorganisation. She has never met criteria for major depressive disorder, generalised anxiety, autism, intellectual disability or substance use disorder. She is in stable employment but routinely works late to catch up. Symptoms are present at home and at work.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: attentive in clinic, organised speech
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.8; FBC normal; urine drug screen negative

lead_in: Which DSM-5-TR criterion is the most important to confirm before making the diagnosis?

options.text: Symptoms present in two or more settings causing functional impairment in current adult life
options.rationale: Multi-setting impairment is required and present here (home and work), but it is not the differentiating step; the developmental onset criterion is the most commonly missed and most often disputed at diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Several inattentive symptoms present before age 12 documented by collateral or records
options.rationale: DSM-5-TR requires several inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms present before age 12; this is the criterion most often inadequately documented in adult presentations and is the strongest differentiator from acquired attentional impairment. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP ADHD Position)

options.text: Five or more inattentive symptoms in the last six months for adults aged 17 years or older
options.rationale: Five-of-nine is correct for adult diagnosis but is straightforward from current symptom report; the developmental criterion is the harder step to verify. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Symptoms not better explained by another mental disorder or substance use disorder
options.rationale: Exclusion criteria are necessary and the history already addresses depression, anxiety, autism and substance use; the development-onset criterion is more often missed. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Symptoms interfere with academic or occupational function as evidenced by collateral report
options.rationale: Functional interference is required and apparent, but does not by itself separate adult-onset attentional complaints from true ADHD; only childhood onset does. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis under DSM-5-TR requires five or more symptoms from inattentive and/or hyperactive-impulsive lists for >=6 months, symptoms present before age 12 (often the hardest criterion to evidence), symptoms present in two or more settings, clear functional impairment, and exclusion of other better explanations. Australian adult ADHD assessment combines structured clinical interview with collateral from a parent or sibling where possible, plus school records when accessible. The 2024 Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD endorses stimulants (lisdexamfetamine, methylphenidate) as first-line pharmacotherapy in adults without contraindication. Comorbidity rates are high; screen routinely for anxiety, depression and substance use. Driving and cardiovascular risk are key safety considerations before stimulant initiation.
key_points: DSM-5-TR adult: 5+ symptoms, onset before age 12, two+ settings, impairment, not better explained
Childhood-onset evidence is the hardest and most-missed criterion
Use collateral and school records to verify
Australian Guidelines 2024: stimulants first-line for adult ADHD
Screen for comorbid anxiety, depression and substance use at diagnosis
explanation.pearls: Adult ADHD symptom threshold is 5/9 inattentive or 5/9 hyperactive-impulsive symptoms, lowered from the paediatric 6/9 to reflect developmental attenuation of overt hyperactivity.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar in a community mental health team seeing a 26-year-old woman with borderline personality disorder confirmed by structured assessment 12 months ago. She has had six self-harm episodes in the past year (superficial cutting) without suicidal intent, recurrent unstable relationships, chronic feelings of emptiness, and recurrent fears of abandonment. She has previously trialled sertraline without benefit and is on no current medication. She is now stable from substance use and has reliable transport to weekly appointments. She wants help to reduce self-harm and emotional storms.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: anxious, organised, no current SI

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line intervention to recommend?

options.text: Commence sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily for emotional dysregulation and labile affect
options.rationale: Mood stabilisers have weak and inconsistent evidence in BPD; the principal indication is psychotherapy and valproate has teratogenic risk in this patient. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline; TGA Valproate Warning 2024)

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nocte for affective instability and review in eight weeks
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not first-line for BPD and SGAs have limited evidence; targeted short courses may be used adjunctively for crises but not as primary treatment. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Twelve months of structured dialectical behaviour therapy with skills group and weekly individual
options.rationale: NHMRC and RANZCP guidance: structured psychotherapy (DBT, MBT, schema-focused, transference-focused) is first-line for BPD with self-harm; DBT has the strongest evidence for reducing self-injury and ED presentations. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline; RANZCP CPG Personality Disorders)

options.text: Refer for inpatient admission to structured ward-based program for six weeks of containment
options.rationale: Brief inpatient admission for BPD is generally discouraged outside acute crisis; longer hospitalisation reinforces dependence and rarely reduces self-harm. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Restart sertraline 100 mg daily and continue with monthly GP review as primary treatment
options.rationale: Sertraline failed previously and SSRIs are not first-line for BPD; psychotherapy is the intervention with proven efficacy. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

explanation.summary: BPD is best managed with structured, time-limited evidence-based psychotherapy delivered by trained clinicians. DBT (12 months, weekly individual plus skills group plus telephone coaching) has the strongest evidence for reducing self-injurious behaviour and ED presentations. Mentalisation-based therapy, schema-focused therapy, and transference-focused therapy are also evidence-based first-line options. Pharmacotherapy plays a limited adjunctive role for specific symptom targets, with no medication approved by TGA for core BPD treatment. Brief crisis admissions are appropriate for safety, but longer admissions are usually counterproductive. The 2024 NHMRC BPD guideline emphasises continuity of care, written crisis plans, and primary care involvement.
key_points: DBT, MBT, schema-focused, TFP = first-line for BPD
DBT has the strongest evidence for self-harm reduction
Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive only - no TGA-approved core medication
Avoid long admissions; use brief crisis admissions only
Written crisis plan should accompany psychotherapy
explanation.pearls: Structured DBT reduces self-injurious behaviour frequency by about 50% over 12 months compared with treatment as usual in randomised trials.

subtopic_detail: ED first-line response in young adult
stem: You are the ED registrar at a regional hospital. A 24-year-old man arrives by ambulance with police following an alleged amphetamine use earlier that evening. He is shouting at staff, pacing, and threatening to leave. He has no known medical conditions. He has not been violent toward staff yet but is making fists. He has no IV access. There is a private interview room available. The mental health and security teams are 3 and 6 minutes away respectively.

data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, dilated pupils, no focal neurology, no obvious injuries

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Allow him to self-discharge under supervision since he has not yet been violent to staff
options.rationale: Allowing exit when he is intoxicated and at risk of harm to self or others would breach duty of care; assessment under Mental Health Act may be required if he refuses to stay. (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline)

options.text: Intramuscular olanzapine 10 mg with midazolam 5 mg now and document the rationale
options.rationale: Parenteral sedation is for severe agitation where de-escalation has failed or is not safe; combining olanzapine with midazolam increases hypotension and respiratory depression risk. (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline)

options.text: Place a Mental Health Act schedule and immediately apply mechanical restraints to the trolley
options.rationale: Restraints are a last resort and should follow failed less-restrictive options; restraints in stimulant-intoxicated patients raise rhabdomyolysis and sudden death risk. (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline)

options.text: Verbal de-escalation in the private room with a single calm clinician and clear space
options.rationale: Australian acute behavioural disturbance guidelines place verbal de-escalation as the first-line response when there is no immediate physical threat and resources allow; lower stimulation and a clear exit reduce the need for restraint. (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline; ACEM Guidelines)

options.text: Wait for the mental health team to attend in 3 minutes before any intervention is started
options.rationale: Initial de-escalation by the treating ED clinician should commence immediately and not wait; the mental health team should join when available. (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline)

explanation.summary: Australian acute behavioural disturbance frameworks (NSW Health, ACEM, RANZCP) emphasise a stepped approach: environmental modification (reduce stimulation, clear space, exits for both patient and staff), verbal de-escalation, oral medication if accepted, then parenteral medication if needed, with mechanical restraint as a last resort. Stimulant intoxication confers higher risk of rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia and sudden death, especially with restraint. When parenteral sedation is required, options include droperidol 10 mg IM, olanzapine 10 mg IM, or midazolam 5 to 10 mg IM, used as monotherapy in preference to combinations to reduce respiratory depression. Continuous monitoring and rapid medical reassessment are mandatory post-sedation.
key_points: Stepped approach: environment, verbal de-escalation, oral, parenteral, restraint last
Stimulant intoxication + restraint = high rhabdomyolysis and sudden death risk
Droperidol 10 mg IM or olanzapine 10 mg IM monotherapy preferred
Avoid combination olanzapine + benzodiazepine (cardiorespiratory depression)
Continuous monitoring required post-sedation
explanation.pearls: Droperidol 10 mg IM achieves adequate sedation within 15 to 30 minutes in 80% of acute behavioural disturbance cases in Australian EDs, with a lower respiratory depression rate than midazolam at equivalent sedation depth.

stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar called to the medical ward for a 42-year-old woman with schizophrenia who has become mute, immobile and resistant to passive limb movement over the past 36 hours. She has not eaten or drunk in 24 hours and is intermittently maintaining bizarre postures with her arm raised. She has not received antipsychotics for one week as she stopped olanzapine on a recent admission. She has no fever and her observations are otherwise stable. There is no rigidity outside the limb posturing and her CK is 220 U/L.

data_table.Examination: Mute, posturing left arm, waxy flexibility on examination, no rigidity, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: CK 220, FBC normal, EUC normal, CRP 4, TSH 2.1, urine drug screen negative
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 15 mg ceased 1 week ago; nil current

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic next step?

options.text: Resume olanzapine 15 mg nocte and add diazepam 5 mg orally three times daily for posturing
options.rationale: Reintroducing antipsychotic during active catatonia carries risk of conversion to NMS; benzodiazepine alone should be tried first with response assessment. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position)

options.text: IV haloperidol 5 mg as urgent rapid tranquillisation for prolonged psychotic posturing
options.rationale: Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and increase the risk of converting to neuroleptic malignant syndrome; first-line is benzodiazepine, not antipsychotic. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position)

options.text: MRI brain with contrast urgently to exclude structural causes before any pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: While neuroimaging is part of catatonia workup, it is not the immediate next step when the lorazepam challenge can simultaneously diagnose and treat; imaging can follow once acute features are addressed. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position)

options.text: Empiric IV dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg with bromocriptine to treat presumed neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: NMS requires fever, autonomic instability, marked rigidity and elevated CK; her temperature, CK, and observations do not support NMS as primary diagnosis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Lorazepam 2 mg IV with observation for response within 10 to 30 minutes as a diagnostic challenge
options.rationale: The lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic in catatonia, with response in 60-80% of cases; partial response supports diagnosis and guides ongoing benzodiazepine titration with ECT as the next step if no response. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position; BFCRS evidence)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is recognised by combinations of immobility, mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism, echolalia and echopraxia, formally assessed using the Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale. It complicates major depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, and a wide range of medical conditions including autoimmune encephalitis, metabolic derangement and substance toxicity. The lorazepam challenge of 1 to 2 mg IV, repeated as needed, both confirms the diagnosis (partial or full response in 60 to 80%) and provides initial treatment. Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and may precipitate NMS, so they are avoided until catatonic features resolve. ECT is the second-line treatment if benzodiazepines fail and is the first-line for malignant catatonia with autonomic instability or hyperthermia. Workup includes EEG, MRI, autoimmune panel and metabolic screen.
key_points: BFCRS quantifies catatonia; >=3 features = diagnosis
Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV is both diagnostic challenge and treatment
Avoid antipsychotics in active catatonia - NMS risk
ECT is second-line; first-line in malignant catatonia
Investigate organic causes: autoimmune encephalitis, metabolic, drug-induced
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia (catatonia with fever, autonomic instability, rigidity and elevated CK) carries a mortality of about 20% if not treated promptly with ECT, and is essentially indistinguishable on presentation from NMS.
